coredata.dc:title: Synergistic effects of CQDs and oxygen vacancies on CeO2 photocatalyst for efficient photocatalytic nitrogen fixation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Mingxiao
Li, Honglin
Wu, Ziyuan
Yu, Zhixin
Tao, Xumei
Huang, Liang
coredata.dc:description: The combination of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with oxygen vacancies (OVs) provides a promising approach to achieving efficient photocatalytic nitrogen fixation. It was by a hydrothermal method that CQDs-modified CeO2 composites (CQDs/CeO2) were synthesized. The synergistic effects of CQDs and OVs on CeO2 photocatalyst enhanced interfacial electron transfer capabilities, thus the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation was significantly enhanced. CQDs/CeO2 exhibited the most superior efficiency in photocatalytic nitrogen fixation, reaching 826 μmol·g−1·h−1, when the addition of CQDs was 200 µL. It was the introduction of CQDs that effectively regulated the concentration of OVs. OVs captured the electrons, thus promoting electron transfer. This enhancement not only improved the electron-hole separation efficiency in CQDs/CeO2 but also greatly promoted the adsorption and activation of N2 by OVs. This work offered a novel strategy for designing efficient photocatalysts for nitrogen fixation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic nitrogen fixation
Carbon quantum dots
Oxygen vacancies
Synergetic mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation procedure of CQDs, CeO2, and CQDs/CeO2.
SEM images of CeO2 (a), 2-CQDs/CeO2 (b), TEM image of 2-CQDs/CeO2 (c), HRTEM image of 2-CQDs/CeO2 (d), EDS mapping of 2-CQDs/CeO2 (e-g).
FT-IR spectra of CQDs, CeO2 and 2-CQDs/CeO2.
Static water contact angles of (a) CeO2 and (b) 2-CQDs/CeO2.
The XPS photoelectron spectrum CQDs, CeO2 and 2-CQDs/CeO2 (a) Survey, (b) Ce 3d, (c) C 1s, (d) O 1s.
(a) ESR spectra and (b) N2-TPD profiles of 1-CQDs/CeO2, 2-CQDs/CeO2 and 3-CQDs/CeO2.
(a) UV–vis, (b) Plots of (αhv)0.5 vs hv of CeO2 and CQDs/CeO2.

(a) NH4 + yield in the dark with N2 input and under visible light irradiation but different atmospheres for 2-CQDs/CeO2 and CeO2; (b) NH4 + yield over different samples under visible light irradiation and N2 atmosphere; (c) Recycling stability test of NH4 + yield for 2-CQDs/CeO2, (d) Comparison with reported photocatalytic nitrogen fixation efficiencies.

(a) Ion chromatogram of CeO2, 1-CQDs/CeO2, 2-CQDs/CeO2 and 3-CQDs/CeO2 (b) 1H NMR spectra of 15NH4 + and 14NH4 + products formed 2-CQDs/CeO2 by photocatalytic nitrogen fixation in 15N2 and 14N2 atmospheres, (c) NH4 + concentrations in different samples after extended photocatalytic nitrogen fixation time under visible light irradiation and N2 atmosphere.
(a) Three-dimensional differential charge density diagram of CQDs/CeO2; (b) Cut-away view of charge density difference in three dimensions.
Mechanism of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation by CQDs/CeO2.
Analysis of Ce Peaks of CeO2 and 2-CQDs/CeO2 by XPS.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a: The amount of Ce3+ was calculated by the formula: (Ce3+)% = (Ce3+)/ (Ce3++ Ce4+) × 100 %.
b: The amount of Ce3+/Ce4+ was calculated by the formula: (Ce3+/ Ce4+)% = (Ce3+)/ (Ce4+) × 100 %.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: O Peak is as of CeO2 and CQDs/CeO2 by XPS and related calculations.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: b: Calculation of OL and OC amount can be substituted into the above formula.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of NH4 + concentrations measured by ion chromatography and Nessler's reagent method.

head.ce:title: Synergistic effects of CQDs and oxygen vacancies on CeO2 photocatalyst for efficient photocatalytic nitrogen fixation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, Shandong, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Electromechanical Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Electromechanical Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, Shandong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The combination of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with oxygen vacancies (OVs) provides a promising approach to achieving efficient photocatalytic nitrogen fixation. It was by a hydrothermal method that CQDs-modified CeO2 composites (CQDs/CeO2) were synthesized. The synergistic effects of CQDs and OVs on CeO2 photocatalyst enhanced interfacial electron transfer capabilities, thus the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation was significantly enhanced. CQDs/CeO2 exhibited the most superior efficiency in photocatalytic nitrogen fixation, reaching 826 μmol·g−1·h−1, when the addition of CQDs was 200 µL. It was the introduction of CQDs that effectively regulated the concentration of OVs. OVs captured the electrons, thus promoting electron transfer. This enhancement not only improved the electron-hole separation efficiency in CQDs/CeO2 but also greatly promoted the adsorption and activation of N2 by OVs. This work offered a novel strategy for designing efficient photocatalysts for nitrogen fixation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia (NH3) is a very important source of raw material in modern agriculture and production industries [1]. Currently, the synthesis of ammonia in the industry sector is primarily carried out according to the Haber-Bosch process. The Haber-Bosch process has harsh conditions (high temperature and high pressure), which leads to significant energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions [2]. At present, electrocatalytic [3,4] and photocatalytic [5,6] have been utilized to synthesize NH3, with its raw materials being H2O and N2. The development of photocatalytic reduction of N2 by adding a certain amount of semiconductor catalysts to the aqueous phase that can be used under ambient conditions with sunlight as the energy source and water as the electron/proton donor is highly promising for development.

Among numerous photocatalysts, cerium dioxide (CeO2) possesses a unique electronic structure (i.e., [Xe] 4f1 5d1 6s2), rendering it highly prone to reversible charge transfer between the Ce4+ and Ce3+ states. Meanwhile, the creation of Ce3+ typically coincides with the occurrence of oxygen vacancy formation [7]. The production of OVs in metal oxides enhances the adsorption and activation of reactants and affects the catalytic efficiency of several processes [8]. This enables CeO2 to trap and activate N2 molecules under reducing or oxidizing conditions. The regulation of the OV sites in metal oxides breaks the scaling relationship, thereby reducing the energy barrier and enhancing photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity [9]. Zhang et al. [10] studied the amorphous engineering of CeO2 and found that CeO2 had abundant OVs. Only when CeO2 had a suitable OV concentration, did it show excellent photocatalytic performance of nitrogen fixation at ambient temperature. However, Because of its low light absorption efficiency, rapid electron-hole recombination, and limited chemisorption of N2 in CeO2, CeO2 showed relatively poor photocatalytic nitrogen fixation efficiency [11,12]. Therefore, the development of innovative strategies was of great significance for the improvement of nitrogen fixation performance based on CeO2.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), with photoluminescence, non-toxicity, unique upconversion luminescence, and other excellent properties, are a new type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials [13–15]. It is the photosensitivity [16] and upconversion effect of CQDs [17] that provide an efficient green method for enhancing carrier separation efficiency and enabling photocatalysts to fully utilize the response to visible light. It has been reported that the introduction of CQDs can form catalytic cavities with metal oxides and promote the adsorption and activation of N2 [18]. Additionally, the introduction of graphene quantum dots made the photoelectrons in metal oxides tend to be transferred to GQDs through hybridization, which made the photoelectrons and holes in the composite material have a higher separation efficiency [19]. The coupling of CQDs with metal oxides played a vital role via comprehensive mechanisms and was expected to serve as ideal photocatalysts for environmental-friendly nitrogen fixation.

In this work, CeO2 with rich OVs was modified with CQDs through a hydrothermal method for efficient photocatalytic nitrogen fixation experiments in a visible light environment. The structure, morphology, chemical composition, and optical properties of CQDs/CeO2 were systematically characterized. The comprehensive mechanism with the synergistic effect of CQDs with OVs was proposed and analyzed. This work proposed a novel photocatalytic nitrogen fixation strategy and broadened photocatalytic mechanisms with the synergistic effect of CQDs with OVs.
Without further purification, all chemicals used were analytically pure.
Characterization details were included in the Supporting Information .

CQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal process. Dissolve 3.15 g of citric acid in 25 mL of deionized water and stir for 15 min to disperse completely. Subsequently, the prepared solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 5 h. The autoclave was naturally cooled to ambient temperature. The solution was filtered with a 0.22 µm needle-type filter to obtain a light yellow solution, denoted as CQDs.

CeO2 was also synthesized by hydrothermal process. 4.3 g of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and 3.2 g of sodium hydroxide were dissolved in 20 mL and 80 mL of deionized water, respectively, and stirred with a magnetic stirrer to obtain two homogeneous solutions, A and B. Solution B was poured into solution A and continued to be stirred for 30 min, resulting in suspension C. Suspension C was then subsequently moved to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 10 h. The suspension was allowed to cool to ambient temperature and separated by centrifugation. The obtained precipitate was washed twice alternately with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol and dried in an oven at 80 °C to obtain CeO2.

CQDs/CeO2 was synthesized as the synthesis of CeO2, with the addition of a certain volume of CQDs into the mixed solution C. The synthesized samples were denoted as 1-CQDs/CeO2, 2-CQDs/CeO2, and 3-CQDs/CeO2 according to the different CQDs volume (100 µL, 200 µL and 300 µL). The preparation procedure of CeO2, CQDs, and CQDs/CeO2 was shown in Fig. 1 .

Photocatalytic nitrogen fixation was conducted within a quartz reactor, employing a 500 W Xenon lamp as a light source under ambient conditions. 250 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed into 0.5 L of deionized water and then transferred to the reactor. Adsorption-desorption equilibrium is established by passing high-purity nitrogen at a flow rate of 80 mL·min−1 in the dark for 30 min. Subsequently, immediately turned on the 500 W xenon lamp and maintained the same nitrogen flow rate for continuous input, with a reaction time of 2 h. After the reaction was completed, the reaction solution and filtered with a 0.22 μL pinhead filter to separate the photocatalyst. The NH4 + concentration was determined by Nessler’s reagent method with a spectrophotometer at 420 nm. The standard curve of absorbance versus NH4 + concentration is shown in Fig.S1 . The NH4 + yield was calculated in the Supporting Information

The crystal structures of CeO2 and 2-CQDs/CeO2 were investigated by XRD as shown in Fig. 2 . CeO2 exhibited obvious diffraction peaks at 2θ = 28.54°, 33.10°, 47.56°, 56.36°, 59.20°, 69.56°, 76.70°, 79.34° and 88.38°, corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), (400), (331), (420) and (422) planes of CeO2 (JCPDS PDF#34-0394) [20,21], respectively. Similarly, the 2-CQDs/CeO2 composite also exhibited nine diffraction peaks, and no diffraction peaks of CQDs were detected in the XRD patterns, indicating that the introduction of CQDs maintained the high purity and crystalline structure of CeO2. This might be due to the low content, high dispersion, and low crystallinity of CQDs in 2-CQDs/CeO2 [22]. Compared with CeO2, the characteristic peaks of 2-CQDs/CeO2 became narrower with enhanced peak intensity, which was attributed to the increase in the crystallinity of 2-CQDs/CeO2 due to the addition of CQDs. The average particle sizes of CeO2 and 2-CQDs/CeO2 were estimated to be 10.8 nm and 12.1 nm, respectively.

The morphology and elements distribution of CeO2 and 2-CQDs/CeO2 were investigated by SEM, TEM, and EDS-mapping, as shown in Fig. 3 . An approximate cubic block structure was observed for CeO2 with an approximate diameter of 11 nm, which was consistent with the XRD results. Compared with CeO2, 2-CQDs/CeO2 didn’t show a significant change in morphology since CQDs were too small to affect the morphology. As the TEM image shows, many black spots were scattered on the surface of CeO2, which demonstrated the successful introduction of CQDs on the surface of CeO2. As exhibited in Fig. 3 (d), the HRTEM image of 2-CQDs/CeO2 displayed lattice spacing of 0.312 nm and 0.189 nm, corresponding to the (111) and (220) crystal planes of monoclinic CeO2 [23,24], respectively; while the lattice spacing of 0.335 nm was assigned to the (002) plane of graphite [25]. The analysis revealed the successful introduction of CQDs and the synthesis of CQDs/CeO2. Meanwhile, It was advantageous for charge transfer that CQDs were uniformly dispersed on CeO2 with intimate interfacial contact. On the selected 2-CQDs/CeO2 area, EDS mapping analysis revealed a good distribution of Ce, C, and O elements.

FT-IR was used to study the functional group composition and structure of CQDs, CeO2 and 2-CQDs/CeO2, as shown in Fig. 4 . The peaks located at 3420.5, 2923.6, 1705.7, 1385.6, and 1216.9 cm−1 in the CQDs were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of the O−H, C−H, C=O, C−H, and C−O bonds [26–29]. The peaks at about 3430 cm−1 in the CeO2 and 2-CQDs/CeO2 were attributed to the O−H which was related to the residual water in the samples [30]. CeO2 and 2-CQDs/CeO2 exhibited a comparable spectrum to bare CeO2 [31] with peaks around 1625, 1386 and 1052 cm−1. The spectrum of CQDs/CeO2 was similar to that of CeO2, indicating that the introduction of CQDs didn’t alter the typical structure of CeO2, in concordance with the findings of XRD characterization. The absorption peaks of 450.3 cm−1 and 433.9 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of Ce−O and Ce−O−C bonds, respectively. As for the spectrum of CQDs/CeO2, the peak shifted toward the side with lower wave numbers compared with that of CeO2, indicating that the mass was increased, since the mass of the vibrating molecule had a negative effect on the frequency of the vibration [32], which indicated the successful introduction of CQDs on CeO2.

The static contact angles of CeO2 and 2-CQDs/CeO2 were measured as shown in Fig. 5 . The static water contact angles for CeO2 and 2-CQDs/CeO2 were 46.60° and 14.59°, respectively. It can be seen that 2-CQDs/CeO2 was more hydrophilic and more likely to be in contact with dissolved nitrogen in water [33]. This was because CQDs contained functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, as shown by FT-IR characterization.

The surface elements and chemical states of CQDs, CeO2, and CQDs/CeO2 were characterized by XPS, as shown in Fig. 6 and Fig.S2 . The XPS spectra in Fig. 6 (a) showed the C 1s and O 1s peaks of CQDs, indicating only C and O elements were present in CQDs. The XPS spectra showed the Ce 3d, C 1s, and O 1s peaks of CeO2 and CQDs/CeO2, indicating the Ce, C, and O elements in CeO2 and CQDs/CeO2.

The high-resolution XPS spectra of Ce 3d was depicted in Fig. 6 (b) and Fig. S2 (b). The characteristic peaks of Ce3d were fitted by four Ce3d5/2 peaks and four Ce3d5/2 peaks, respectively represented by λ(labeled as λ, λ', λ'' and λ''') and γ(labeled as γ, γ', γ'' and γ'''). The peaks λ' and γ' were attributed to Ce3d5/2 and Ce3d3/2 in Ce3+ state, and the remaining peaks represent the Ce4+ state [21]. A slight shift towards lower binding energies in the Ce 3d peaks of 2-CQDs/CeO2 compared to CeO2 was noteworthy. This shift was attributed to the transfer of electrons from CQDs to CeO2 in 2-CQDs/CeO2 [34,35]. Table 1 and Tab.S1 detailed the area of Ce3+ and Ce4+ by XPS, and the Ce3+/(Ce3++Ce4+) ratio. The co-existence of Ce3+ and Ce4+ inside the CeO2 and CQDs/CeO2 indicated the existence of OVs, which were beneficial for the adsorption of N2 molecules and the separation of photo-generated carriers. It can be seen that the introduction of CQDs increased the relative Ce3+ content. This was because the electrons provided by the CQDs were usually transferred to the Ce atoms, thus promoting the reduction of more Ce4+ to Ce3+. It was the formation of OVs and unsaturated chemical bonds that accompanied the reduction of Ce, enabling stronger chemisorption to facilitate catalytic reactions [36].

Fig. 6 (c) and Fig. S2 (c) illustrated the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s for CQDs and 2-CQDs/CeO2. For CQDs, the C 1s spectrum exhibited three peaks at 284.8, 286.6, and 289.06 eV, which could be ascribed to C–C/C=C, C-O, and O=C-O bonds [37]. The C 1s peaks of 2-CQDs/CeO2 were similar to that of CQDs, indicating the successful introduction of CQDs, in agreement with the FT-IR results. The existence of hydroxyl functional groups determined the hydrophilic property of 2-CQDs/CeO2.

The high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s was depicted in Fig. 6 (d) and Fig. S2 (d). For CeO2 and CQDs/CeO2 samples, the characteristic peaks of O 1s were fitted with three peaks, corresponding to O2– species in the lattice (OL), oxygen vacancy (OV), and chemisorbed or dissociated oxygen species (OC) in the water molecule [38]. However, for CQDs, the O 1s spectrum exhibited only one peak at 532.68 eV, which was ascribed to the chemisorbed or dissociated oxygen species (OC). The amount of OV was calculated with the OV/(OC+OV+OL) ratio based on the peak areas of these O species, as shown in Table 2 . The amount of Ov on the surface of 2-CQDs/CeO2 was higher than that on CeO2 (31.87 % vs 23.58 %) and other CQDs/CeO2 samples (31.87 % vs 24.4 % and 18.73 %). Oxygen vacancies can provide active sites to chemisorb nitrogen molecules [8] and also can capture electrons [39]. The moderate combination of CQDs promoted the formation of OVs, which in turn accepted electron transfer from CQDs, to cooperatively promoted the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation reaction.

ESR spectrum was carried out to prove the existence of OVs, as shown in Fig. 7
(a). At g = 2.003, the ESR spectrum exhibited distinct resonance signals indicative of the presence of OVs. The intensity of these signals directly quantified the OV concentration in the corresponding catalysts. The signal intensities were as follows: 2-CQDs/CeO2, 1-CQDs/CeO2, CeO2, and 3-CQDs/CeO2, suggesting a regulatory role of CQDs on OVs. This finding was consistent with the OV content order results obtained by XPS. The N2 adsorption performance of the photocatalyst was detected by nitrogen temperature-programmed desorption (N2-TPD) as shown in Fig. 7 (b), and the desorption peak before 200 °C was considered to be physisorption. The chemical absorption peaks of 1-CQDs/CeO2, 2-CQDs/CeO2 and 3-CQDs/CeO2 were located at 600 °C, 624 °C and 612 °C, respectively. 2-CQDs/CeO2 had the strongest chemisorption activity. Combined with the ESR results, it was found that oxygen vacancies could induce nitrogen chemisorption. It can be inferred that the oxygen vacancy can trap electrons, and the electrons on the CQDs were captured by OVs.

The optical property and light absorption ability of CeO2 and CQDs/CeO2 samples were analyzed using UV–Vis, and the band gap energies were estimated based on plots of (αhv)0.5 vs photon energy (hv), as shown in Fig. 8 . CQDs/CeO2 samples exhibited slightly increased ultraviolet responsibility in the wavelength region of 200–350 nm, which was attributed to the upconversion properties of CQDs. The upconversion properties of CQDs could convert low-energy, long-wavelength visible light into high-energy, short-wavelength ultraviolet light. Therefore, the introduction of CQDs was more conducive to the activation of ultraviolet-responded photocatalysts under visible irradiation. According to the Tauc’s plots (Fig. 8 (b), Tauc’s equation was provided in the Supporting Information ), the band gap energies of CeO2, 1-CQDs/CeO2, 2-CQDs/CeO2, and 3-CQDs/CeO2 were estimated to be 2.65, 2.649, 2.68, and 2.59 eV, respectively. It was found that the introduction of CQDs had little effect on the band gap.

To further analyze the optical properties of CeO2 and 2-CQDs/CeO2, PL was carried out, as shown in Fig. 9 . The broad emission for CeO2 and 2-CQDs/CeO2 occurred between 360 and 800 nm, corresponding to deep energy level emission from charge carrier complexation in the materials [40]. The higher the intensity of the PL spectrum, the more electron and hole complexation dominated [41]. It was the weaker PL intensity exhibited by 2-CQDs/CeO2 that indicated the significant suppression of the recombination rate of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, thereby benefiting the improvement of the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation efficiency.

The interferences of environmental factors were investigated for 2-CQDs/CeO2 and CeO2 as shown in Fig. 10
(a). It was found that NH4 + yields in the dark with N2 input were only 0 μmol·g−1·h−1 for 2-CQDs/CeO2 and CeO2. And NH4 + yield was hardly increased with Ar input under visible light irradiation. The NH4 + yields could reach 632 and 826 μmol·g−1·h−1 for CeO2 and 2-CQDs/CeO2, respectively, under visible light irradiation and high-purity nitrogen input. Therefore, it can be determined that NH4 + was generated by photocatalyst under visible light irradiation with nitrogen providing nitrogen source.

Fig. 10 (b) compared the average NH4 + yield over different samples under visible light irradiation and high-purity nitrogen input. It can be seen that CQDs/CeO2 composites exhibited better photocatalytic nitrogen fixation efficiency than CeO2. The improvement supported the role of CQDs in promoting the formation of OVs and providing electrons to Ce atoms, thus promoting the transfer of photogenerated carriers. Also, it was worth noting that CQD content affected the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation efficiency greatly. At an addition of 200 µL, the combination of 2-CQDs/CeO2 demonstrated the most efficient photocatalytic nitrogen fixation efficiency, achieving a rate of 826 μmol·g−1·h−1. It can be found that further increased CQDs content led to a lower photocatalytic nitrogen fixation efficiency than that of 2-CQDs/CeO2. It can be concluded from the XPS Ce 3d and O 1s maps that the excessive addition of CQDs inhibited the conversion of Ce4+ to Ce3+, resulting in the reduction of oxygen vacancy as the active site for photocatalytic nitrogen fixation, thus weakening the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance. In addition, the accumulation of excess CQDs may hinder light absorption [42].

The stability and sustainability of 2-CQDs/CeO2 were investigated through cyclic experiments of nitrogen fixation reactions under the same photocatalytic conditions, with the results presented in Fig. 10 (c). It can be seen that there was no apparent deactivation in photocatalytic nitrogen fixation efficiency after four recycles, implying the stability of 2-CQDs/CeO2. Fig. 10 (d) and Tab.S(2, 3) showed the comparison of the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation efficiencies of different catalysts, and 2-CQDs/CeO2 showed better photocatalytic nitrogen fixation efficiencies.

The ionic components and concentrations in the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation reaction solution were further detected by ion chromatography. The ion chromatogram (Fig. 11 ) showed only the NH4 + peak, indicating that the photocatalytic reaction had converted N2 to NH3. The NH4 + concentration determined by ion chromatography was shown in Table 3 and there was little error with the NH4 + concentration calculated from the standard curve of NH4 + concentration-absorbance. To confirm the source of the synthetic NH3, an isotope labeling experiment was performed with 15N2 as the N2 source. The 1H NMR spectra exhibited a triplet coupling for 14NH4 + and a doublet coupling for 15NH4 + as shown in Fig. 11 (b). It was strongly demonstrated that the source of photocatalytic synthesis of NH3 was N2. To further explore the accuracy of the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation capacity and nitrogen fixation yield of the samples, the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation time was extended. As shown in Fig. 11 (c), higher concentrations of NH4 + can be obtained with the extension of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation time. Detailed ammonium ion concentrations are shown in Tab.S5 of the Supporting Information .

To further determine the stability of the catalyst structure, the 2-CQDs/CeO2 catalyst after multiple cycles was analyzed by SEM and XRD as shown in Fig.S3 . From the XRD pattern, it can be seen that there was no significant change in the crystal peak, indicating that the crystal structure was not changed during the cyclic experiment. At the same time, the morphology of the catalyst was not affected by cyclic experiments. The recovered 2-CQDs/CeO2 catalyst was analyzed by XPS ( Fig.S4 ) and compared with the fresh catalyst. The XPS spectra of the C 1s showed that CQDs still existed stable after cycling. The OVs content (Tab.S4) of the recovered 2-CQDs/CeO2 catalyst did not significantly decrease, and the OVs after the reaction remained stable.

The electron transfer paths of the CQDs/CeO2 composite were determined using the DFT first calculation principle for the checkpoint charge density calculation and analysis, as shown in Fig. 12 . It can be seen a clear electron transfer trend occurred between CQDs and CeO2 since the cyan part represented electron depletion while the yellow part represented electron accumulation. The electrons were depleted in the CQDs layer and accumulated in the CeO2 layer, indicating the electron transferred from CQDs to CeO2. This fully validated the conclusion of the electron transfer pathway inferred from the XPS Ce 3d high-definition resolution mapping. The electron transfer path of the CQDs/CeO2 composite was also evidenced by the charge density distribution map (Fig. 12 (b)) at the same position as Fig. 12 (a), where blue color represented electron loss and red color represented electron accumulation. The electrons generated by the CQDs appeared to tend to transfer toward the CeO2 layer. The electrons accumulated mainly at the oxygen sites of CeO2, and electron loss occurred near the Ce sites due to the unique electronic structure of Ce (i.e., [Xe] 4f1 5d1 6s2). It was worth noting that a catalytic cavity was formed between the CQDs and CeO2, where the accumulation of electrons generated by the CQDs occurred, and the CeO2 accepted the electrons accumulated here, creating an effective built-in electric field.

Drawing from the analysis provided above, the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation mechanism of CQDs/CeO2 was proposed (Fig. 13 ). Normally, electrons in the valence band of CeO2 can be excited by visible light to transfer into the conduction band and generate holes on the valence band. The photoexcited electrons and holes moved erratically, partly compounded, and partly transferred to the CeO2 surface for photocatalytic reactions. After the introduction of CQDs, the energy band structure of the photocatalysts did not change significantly, which showed that the energy band structure was not the primary factor for enhancing nitrogen fixation efficiency. However, it was shown that CQDs could be effectively converted into high-energy, short-wavelength UV light to further promote photocatalytic excitation for N2 reduction. Combined with the characterization results, it can be seen that CQDs not only significantly inhibited electron-hole complexation, but also promoted the reduction ability of Ce and elevated the oxygen vacancy content. The defect structures formed by oxygen vacancies can provide electron-trapping centers [43]. The formation of OVs promoted the effective separation of electrons and holes and improved the utilization of photogenerated electrons and holes. Ce3+ as the active site was the main reason for the formation of OVs, which adsorbed nitrogen and captured electrons for nitrogen activation. The introduction of CQDs positively regulated the (Ce3+)% and OVs content, which greatly enhanced the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation efficiency. The synergistic mechanism of CQDs and OVs was fully elucidated here. Overall, the synergistic effects of CQDs and OVs on the photoexcitation and absorption properties, as well as the electron transfer and separation characteristics, improved the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation efficiency of CQDs/CeO2.

In summary, an efficient CQDs/CeO2 composite was synthesized via the hydrothermal method. The synergistic effects of CQDs and oxygen vacancies on CeO2 photocatalyst enhanced interfacial electron transfer capabilities. The photocatalytic nitrogen fixation efficiency of CQDs/CeO2 reached the maximum of 826 μmol·g−1·h−1 when the addition amount of CQDs was 200 µL. The introduction of CQDs could effectively regulate the concentration of OVs, which trapped the electrons and thus induced the electron transfer and improved the transfer rate of electrons and holes and holes in the CQDs/CeO2 composite. The OVs received electrons generated from CQDs and CeO2 by light excitation, which activated the adsorbed N2 and enhanced the photocatalytic performance. In addition, the CQDs can efficiently convert low-energy and short-wavelength visible light into high-energy and short-wavelength UV light, and the electrons generated from the CQDs by photoexcitation not only made up for the electrons lost due to the electron-hole complexation in CeO2, but also can be captured by the OVs, which further promoted the photocatalytic excitation for the N2. In this study, a new method for the modification of CeO2 with CQDs was proposed to improve the performance of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation, providing valuable opinions for the development of this field.

Mingxiao Cheng: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Validation. Honglin Li: Project administration. Ziyuan Wu: Project administration. Zhixin Yu: Project administration. Xumei Tao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Liang Huang: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2024.129299.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
